items.question: Who needs to recognize underfitting vs overfitting?
items.answer: ML engineers, data scientists, and students building supervised models need these diagnostics before deployment — anyone who picks model complexity, regularization, or training duration using train and validation metrics.

items.question: What is underfitting?
items.answer: Underfitting is when a model is too simple to capture trainable structure: both training and validation error stay high, often because capacity is too low, features are missing, or regularization is too strong.

items.question: When should you suspect overfitting?
items.answer: Suspect overfitting when training error is low but validation error is much higher — a large train–validation gap — especially after increasing model complexity, training many epochs, or working with small datasets.

items.question: Where do you see underfitting and overfitting in experiments?
items.answer: They appear in learning curves (error vs training set size), validation curves (error vs complexity hyperparameters), and epoch-by-epoch train/val loss logs during iterative training.

items.question: Why is validation error the selection metric?
items.answer: Training error always improves with more capacity and memorization; validation error estimates performance on unseen data — it rises when the model overfits and stays high when the model underfits.

items.question: How do you fix underfitting vs overfitting?
items.answer: Fix underfitting by increasing capacity, adding informative features, or easing excessive regularization. Fix overfitting with more data, stronger regularization, simpler models, or early stopping when validation error stops improving.

items.question: Underfitting vs overfitting — how do train and validation errors compare?
items.answer: Underfitting: training and validation error are both high and relatively close. Overfitting: training error is low but validation error is much higher — a large generalization gap.

items.question: Underfitting vs high bias — what is the relationship?
items.answer: Underfitting is the practical symptom of high bias (model too restrictive). Bias owns the formal E[ŷ] − f*(x) term; this topic owns the train+val-high diagnostic and capacity fixes.

items.question: Overfitting vs high variance — what is the relationship?
items.answer: Overfitting is the practical symptom of high variance (sensitivity to the training sample). Variance owns Var(ŷ) in the decomposition; this topic owns the low-train/high-val gap and mitigation workflow.

items.question: Cause: model complexity far too low for the true pattern. Effect?
items.answer: Training and validation error remain high — underfitting — and adding moderate amounts of data does little; capacity or features must increase.

items.question: Cause: very flexible model on a small noisy training set without regularization. Effect?
items.answer: Training error drops sharply while validation error stays high or worsens — overfitting — and predictions become unstable across retrained models.
